topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights hummed a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. Sarah, the new hire, was asking the same foundational questions for the third time in as many minutes. I tapped my pen sharply against the training manual, the sound echoing too loudly in the otherwise quiet office cubicle. "Look, I've shown you the onboarding documents. Just absorb this," I snapped, my jaw tight. I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes. Every minute spent repeating the same steps felt like a personal waste of valuable time. I wanted to just hand her the keys and walk away. "Can you at least follow along without needing a full recap every five minutes?"

[story 2]

My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the conference table. I watched Mark fumble with the software interface, his brow furrowed in confusion. It was the same stumbling block I’d navigated weeks ago, and the sheer repetition was starting to grate on my nerves. "Okay, let's try that sequence again," I said, my voice pitched a little higher than intended. I watched him nod slowly, then immediately backtrack. I sighed, a deep, ragged sound that escaped before I could stop it. *This is taking forever. Why can't she just pick this up?* I leaned back in my chair, deliberately crossing my arms, waiting for a breakthrough that just wouldn't materialize.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the open-plan workspace, my footsteps quick and uneven on the polished floor. The clock on the wall seemed to mock me with its steady, relentless ticking. Liam was struggling to locate a specific file, a task that should have taken him less than a minute. I stopped abruptly beside his desk, leaning in close enough that I could smell the faint scent of stale coffee. "It’s right there, Liam. See the folder labeled 'Q3 Reports'?" My tone was clipped, bordering on sharp. I watched as he finally spotted it, a flicker of relief crossing his face, but I didn't let myself relax. I straightened up too quickly, the tension in my shoulders refusing to release. "Don't let that happen again," I muttered, turning away before he could respond.
